questions.question_text: In Arizona, which type of lien always has first priority over all others?
distractors: Mechanic's liens by work start date
questions.explanation: Property tax liens have first priority (superior to all other liens) in Arizona. This means unpaid property taxes must be satisfied before any mortgage, mechanic's lien, or HOA assessment.

questions.question_text: In Arizona, a mechanic's lien must be filed within how many days of project completion?
questions.explanation: Arizona allows 120 days from completion of the work to file a mechanic's lien. The lien's priority relates back to when construction began (not the filing date), which can make mechanic's liens very powerful.

questions.question_text: A mechanic's lien in Arizona has priority dating back to:
questions.correct_answer: The start of construction, not the filing date
distractors: The date the lien was filed
The date the contract was signed
The date the work was completed
questions.explanation: Arizona mechanic's liens have "relate-back" priority to the start of construction. This means a mechanic's lien filed after a mortgage was recorded could still have priority if construction began before the mortgage.

questions.question_text: Which deed provides the broadest protection against title defects?
questions.explanation: A warranty deed warrants against ALL defects in title, including those that arose before the grantor's ownership. A special warranty deed only covers the grantor's ownership period. A quitclaim deed provides no warranties at all.

questions.question_text: A grantor transfers property using a quitclaim deed. What warranties does the buyer receive?
distractors: Warranty against the grantor's own actions
Full warranty against all title defects
Limited warranty for the prior 10 years
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed transfers whatever interest the grantor has (if any) with no warranties. The buyer receives no guarantees about the quality of title. This is the riskiest deed type for the grantee.

questions.question_text: Under Arizona law, what is the minimum number of lots to qualify as a subdivision?
questions.explanation: Arizona defines a subdivision as any land divided into 6 or more lots for sale or lease. Five or fewer lots is considered unsubdivided land and requires an Affidavit of Disclosure rather than a public report.

questions.question_text: A buyer in a planned community requests HOA governing documents from the seller. How quickly must they be provided?
questions.explanation: Arizona's Planned Community Act requires sellers to provide HOA information (governing documents, financial statements, disclosure summary) within 10 days of the buyer's request.

questions.question_text: An Arizona HOA wants to prohibit homeowners from installing solar panels. Under state law, this is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited — HOAs cannot ban solar panels
distractors: Allowed if approved by 75% of homeowners
Legal as long as written notice is given
questions.explanation: Arizona limits certain HOA powers. HOAs cannot prohibit solar panels, political signs (within 71 days of an election), or flagpoles in certain circumstances. These protections are established by state statute.

questions.question_text: CC&Rs (Covenants, Conditions, and Restrictions) in an Arizona subdivision are said to:
questions.correct_answer: Run with the land and bind future owners
distractors: Apply only to the original purchaser
Become void when the HOA board changes
questions.explanation: CC&Rs run with the land, meaning they bind all current and future property owners in the subdivision. They are recorded against the property and transfer automatically when the property is sold.

questions.question_text: What must a buyer receive before signing a purchase contract for a lot in an Arizona subdivision?
distractors: A building permit from the city
questions.explanation: Buyers must receive the ADRE public report before signing a purchase contract in a subdivision. The report discloses legal description, utilities, roads, CC&Rs, financial arrangements, water supply, and potential hazards.

questions.question_text: How long must open, notorious, hostile, and continuous use last to create a prescriptive easement in Arizona?
questions.explanation: In Arizona, a prescriptive easement requires 10 years of open, notorious, hostile, and continuous use. This is longer than some states but shorter than others. The use must be without the owner's permission.

questions.question_text: A neighbor has been using a path across your Arizona property openly for 12 years without your permission. They may have acquired:
distractors: No legal interest in the property
questions.explanation: With 12 years of open, notorious, hostile, and continuous use (exceeding Arizona's 10-year requirement), the neighbor may have acquired a prescriptive easement. This creates a legal right to continue using the path.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between an easement appurtenant and an easement in gross?
questions.correct_answer: Appurtenant runs with the land; in gross is personal
distractors: Appurtenant is temporary; in gross is permanent
Appurtenant is written; in gross is implied
Appurtenant is for utilities; in gross is for access
questions.explanation: An easement appurtenant benefits a specific property (dominant estate) and runs with the land when sold. An easement in gross is a personal right held by an individual or entity (like a utility company) unconnected to land ownership.

questions.question_text: A property is landlocked with no road access. The owner can seek which type of easement?
distractors: No easement is available for landlocked property
questions.explanation: An easement by necessity can be created when a property is landlocked and needs access over an adjoining property. This type of easement arises from the circumstances of the original land division.

questions.question_text: Which of the following can create an easement in Arizona?
questions.correct_answer: Express grant, implication, necessity, or prescription
distractors: Only by written agreement between parties
Only by court order or eminent domain
Only by recording with the county recorder
questions.explanation: Arizona recognizes four methods of creating easements: express grant (written agreement), implication (circumstances suggest it), necessity (landlocked property), and prescription (adverse use for 10+ years).
